cards.text: Using exception classes instead of basic types allows you to include {{c1::additional data}} and {{c2::member functions}} with the exception, making them more descriptive and useful.

cards.question: How do you throw and catch this custom exception?
cards.answer:
throw ArrayException("Invalid index");
catch (const ArrayException& e) { std::cout << e.getError(); }
cards.explanation: Throw by value, catch by const reference. The exception object can carry data (error message) and provide member functions.

cards.text: When catching exceptions with inheritance, a catch handler for a {{c1::base class}} will also catch exceptions of any {{c2::derived class}} type.

cards.question: Which catch block executes if DerivedError is thrown?
cards.explanation: Catch blocks are checked in order. Since DerivedError is-a BaseError, the base class handler matches first. The derived handler is unreachable.

cards.front: When catching exceptions from an inheritance hierarchy, what order should catch blocks be in?
cards.back: Most derived classes first, base classes last. This ensures specific exceptions are handled before more general ones.

cards.text: The C++ standard library provides {{c1::std::exception}} as a base class for all standard exceptions. It defines a virtual member function {{c2::what()}} that returns a C-string describing the error.

cards.question: What's the benefit of inheriting from std::exception?
cards.answer: Your exception can be caught by catch (const std::exception&) handlers along with standard exceptions, providing a unified error handling interface.
cards.explanation: Inheriting from std::exception allows your custom exceptions to be caught alongside standard library exceptions using polymorphism.

cards.text: The recommended practice is to {{c1::throw by value}} and {{c2::catch by (const) reference}}. This avoids slicing and unnecessary copying.

cards.question: What's wrong with catching by value here?
cards.answer: Catching by value causes object slicing - the DerivedError is sliced to BaseError, losing the extraInfo member and any derived class behavior.
cards.explanation: Catching by value copies the exception and slices it to the catch parameter type. Use const reference to preserve the full derived object.

Exception hierarchies enable flexible error handling:
  • Derive all custom exceptions from std::exception
  • Create specific exception types for different errors
  • Catch specific types for targeted handling
  • Catch base types for general handling
  • Order catch blocks from most to least specific

cards.front: What does the std::exception::what() function return?
cards.back: It returns a const char* (C-string) containing a description of the exception. It's a virtual function meant to be overridden by derived classes.

cards.text: When catching exceptions from an inheritance hierarchy, place catch blocks for {{c1::derived}} classes before catch blocks for {{c2::base}} classes to avoid unreachable code.
